items.q: What is the difference between an arithmetic and a geometric sequence?
items.correct: Arithmetic has a constant difference between terms; geometric has a constant ratio.
items.misconception: Arithmetic sequences involve only addition and geometric sequences only multiplication, so 1, 2, 4 is arithmetic.
items.random_wrong: Arithmetic sequences only use whole numbers and geometric sequences use decimals.
items.why: Students confuse 'arithmetic' (sounding like 'maths') with addition-only; they don't link 'geometric' with multiplicative ratio.

items.correct: No — it approaches 0 as the limit but no term is ever exactly 0.
items.misconception: Eventually a term will be so small it equals 0.
items.random_wrong: It reaches 0 at the millionth term.
items.why: Students see terms 'getting really small' and assume they reach zero; they don't grasp the idea of approaching a limit without reaching it.

items.q: What is the relationship between the gradients of two perpendicular lines?
items.correct: Their product is -1 — each is the negative reciprocal of the other.
items.misconception: They are equal because perpendicular lines are 'similar' in direction.
items.random_wrong: One is the negative of the other (e.g. 3 and -3).
items.why: Students remember 'negative' but drop the 'reciprocal' part, leading them to negate without flipping.

items.q: The 10th term of the arithmetic sequence 5, 8, 11, 14, ... is:
items.why: Correct answer: 32. “35” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The sum to infinity of 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + ... is:
items.why: Correct answer: 16. “32” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Find the sum of the first 20 terms of the arithmetic sequence with a = 3 and d = 2.
items.why: The real figure is 440. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
